answer:
No, these are not the same kind of argument, though they are structurally analogous within a shared natural rights framework.[^1]

Comte's core claim is that *natural liberty*—the unobstructed exercise of human faculties—is the prior condition for all rights and duties, from which property rights derivatively emerge: humans cannot legitimately own other humans (as that negates liberty), but they rightfully appropriate unowned things via free labor and occupation, making the products of their faculties their exclusive property.[^2][^3] Property is thus grounded in and delimited by liberty; it presupposes free persons, not vice versa.

Fichte's argument, by contrast, is that *freedom of thought*—an inalienable manifestation of personhood and moral autonomy—is a natural right that princes/society cannot legitimately infringe via censorship or edict, as all political authority derives solely from a social contract to *protect* (not alienate) such rights.[^4][^5] Legitimate authority is thus grounded in and delimited by this specific freedom; suppressing it voids the contract's basis and renders rule tyrannical.

**Similarities**: Both are deductive arguments from natural law/personhood (inalienable human faculties/liberties as foundational) to institutional legitimacy (property as extension of liberty; authority as protector of liberty). Both reject slavery/domination as violations of natural liberty and insist institutions must respect it as a precondition.[^6]

**Differences**: Comte grounds a *private right* (property over things) in *general liberty* (over one's person/faculties), emphasizing economic/occupational derivation. Fichte grounds a *public limit* on authority in *specific liberty* (thought/communication), emphasizing political contract theory and anti-censorship. Comte's is more material/appropriation-focused; Fichte's more ideal/moral-autonomy-focused.

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, Chapter 2 [full chapter](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-consideree-comme-une-condition-de-le/translation/).

[^2]: Ibid., [p-2b4fad](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-consideree-comme-une-condition-de-le/translation/#p-2b4fad), [p-60f07c](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-consideree-comme-une-condition-de-le/translation/#p-60f07c).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, Chapter 3 [full chapter](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/), esp. [p-86c544](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-86c544) ("the principle of liberty is... that the products of his labors belong only to him").

[^4]: Johann Gottlieb Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens*, Chapter 2 [full chapter](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/), esp. [p-5ec8d2](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-5ec8d2) ("freedom... is the necessary condition under which alone he can say: I am"), [p-e62dae](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-e62dae) ("freedom of thought... belongs among [inalienable human rights]").

[^5]: Ibid., [p-be3fae](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-be3fae) ("The Prince... derives his rights from a transfer by society"), [p-44e1de](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-44e1de) ("Prince, you have no right to suppress our freedom of thought").

[^6]: Cf. Comte Chapter 2 [p-a75b90](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-consideree-comme-une-condition-de-le/translation/#p-a75b90); Fichte Chapter 2 [p-900390](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-900390).

args.query: "freedom" property OR liberty OR "natural liberty" OR "human freedom"
"freedom of thought" OR "thought" freedom OR "freedom of the press" fichte
